coredata.dc:title: Carbonized polymer dots-based composites with solution and solid-state room temperature phosphorescence for white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yichuan
Long, Qianhui
Ding, Yanjun
Huang, Chuan
Guo, Mingming
coredata.dc:description: Although carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have witnessed significant developments in the field room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, how to improve the RTP lifetime remains a challenge. Herein, Arginine (Arg) and phosphoric acid were used to synthesize Arg-CPDs and then it was introduced into the SiO2 network where the C–Si bonds were created between the two components. The RTP emission of over 10 s can be found both in the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution and anhydrous Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites. The anhydrous composites displayed an RTP lifetime of 543 ms, which was more than 3 times higher than that (149 ms) of the matrix-free Arg-CPDs materials. It is the result of the rigid SiO2 network inhibiting the vibration of light-emitting groups of Arg-CPDs and isolating them for preventing the quenching of triplet excitons. Besides, the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite was successfully applied to prepare white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) and suggested the promising potential in the WLED illumination application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Composites
Room temperature phosphorescent
White light-emitting diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite-based WLEDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. SWU-XDJH202314 , SWU-KR22041 and SWU118113 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (No. CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0602 ). The authors would like to thank Tianyang Hu from Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the XPS and phosphorescence analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation schematic for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a∼f) SEM and EDS element mapping images, (g) TEM image, and (h) XRD spectrum of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) FTIR spectrum of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite, (b) full XPS spectrum of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite, (c ∼ e) high-resolution XPS spectra for C 1 s (c), O 1 s and Si 2p (e) of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite, (f) structure schematic of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorbance, fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 aqueous solution, (b) 3D fluorescence contour maps of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 aqueous solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Digital photos of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution under daylight, UV light on and off, (b) fluorescence (FL) and RTP emission spectra of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution excited at 365 nm, (c) RTP decay curve of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 powder, (b) FL emission and RTP emission spectra of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 powder excited at 365 nm, (c) FL emission and RTP emission color coordinates of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 powder in CIE coordinate diagram, (d) RTP decay curve of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 powder excited at 365 nm.

(a) Emission spectra of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2-based WLEDs, the inset: photographs of WLEDs under working; (b) CIE coordinates of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2-based WLEDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Carbonized polymer dots-based composites with solution and solid-state room temperature phosphorescence for white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft-Matter Material Chemistry and Function Manufacturing, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft-Matter Material Chemistry and Function Manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft-Matter Material Chemistry and Function Manufacturing, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have witnessed significant developments in the field room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, how to improve the RTP lifetime remains a challenge. Herein, Arginine (Arg) and phosphoric acid were used to synthesize Arg-CPDs and then it was introduced into the SiO2 network where the C–Si bonds were created between the two components. The RTP emission of over 10 s can be found both in the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution and anhydrous Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites. The anhydrous composites displayed an RTP lifetime of 543 ms, which was more than 3 times higher than that (149 ms) of the matrix-free Arg-CPDs materials. It is the result of the rigid SiO2 network inhibiting the vibration of light-emitting groups of Arg-CPDs and isolating them for preventing the quenching of triplet excitons. Besides, the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite was successfully applied to prepare white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) and suggested the promising potential in the WLED illumination application.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have witnessed explosive developments in recent years due to the large stokes shift and high signal-to-noise ratio, exhibiting the potential applications in anti-counterfeiting, data encryption, bioimaging, sensing, and so on [1–4]. Traditional RTP materials are usually metal-doped inorganic or metal-organic complexes in which the heavy metal ions (such as Ir3+ or Pt2+) facilitate the intersystem crossing (ISC) and thus enhance spin-forbidden phosphorescence [5]. Heavy metals have the intrinsic shortcomings of scarcity, high cost and element toxicity. To address this issue, great breakthroughs have been achieved in the preparation of RTP materials with inorganic complexes or metal‐free organic compounds [6]. However, these metal-free RTP materials are typically associated with complex preparation processes, harsh preparation conditions, and high reagent costs [7,8]. Therefore, it is still a formidable task to develop a new type of RTP material with a long-lived luminescence time via simple, low-cost and low-toxicity preparation methods.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with a spherical or quasi-spherical core-shell structure [9,10], including carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), etc. [11,12]. They have aroused increasing attention due to their low toxicity, high chemical stability, and environment-friendliness [13,14]. Typically, it is difficult for CDs to present phosphorescence at room temperature. The reason lies in the fact that the light-emitting functional groups on the surface of CDs are very vulnerable to the environment (i.e., oxygen and water) and trigger the quenching of triplet excitons [15]. To achieve an effective RTP, several strategies have been proposed to facilitate the ISC process and immobilize triplet states by introducing intermolecular interactions between CDs and rigid matrices [16–18]. For example, Zhou et al. [17] adopted a co-crystallization strategy with cyanuric acid (CA) to modulate the emissions of CDs and the resultant CDs@CA composite produces a phosphorescence lifetime of 496 ms. However, the phosphorescence lifetime is still unsatisfying and the fabrication of CD-based composites with ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime remains a great challenge.

Our previous work employed a microwave-assisted heating method to synthesize nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) co-doped CPDs (Arginine (Arg)-CPDs) and the matrix-free Arg-CPDs exhibit a phosphorescence lifetime of 149 ms in the ambient conditions [19]. In the present work, the phosphorescence lifetime (543 ms) was remarkably promoted by introducing a rigid SiO2 network that facilitates the intersystem crossing (ISC) process and restricts the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons. The Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite was successfully applied to prepare white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) based on their solid-state luminescence properties. The present work provided a simple but effective method to improve the phosphorescence lifetime and demonstrated their promising application potential in the WLED illumination field.

l-Arginine (Arg, purity 99.0 %), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, purity 98.0 %), and ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, G.R., 25–28 %) were bought from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Company (China). Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) was bought from ChuanDong Chemical Reagent Company (China). All reagents, including H3PO4 and deionized water, were used as received without any further purification.

Fig. 1 displays a schematic illustration of the preparation procedures for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite. Typically, the aqueous Arg solution was mixed with H3PO4, and the mixture was heated by a micromove oven (700 W). The obtained gel-like product was dissolved, centrifuged, filtered and dialyzed to remove impurities. Afterward, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain the Arg-CPDs powders. The preparation details about Arg-CPDs were reported in the previous work [19]. The Arg-CPDs powder (40 mg) was further dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and then TEOS was drop-wisely added into the Arg-CPDs solution with continuous stirring. Subsequently, NH3·H2O was added to regulate the pH value around 9. NH3·H2O also plays a role of a morphological catalyst to control the particle dimensions and facilitates the formation of SiO2 network [20]. The mixture was stirred (600 r/min) for 5 h at 100 °C. After the reaction, the supernatant was collected and subjected to dialysis (MWCO:1000 Da) for 48 h to remove impurities. Finally, the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite powders can be obtained after freeze-drying.

The morphology of the sample was observed by field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos F200X) and scanning electronic microscope (SEM, GeminiSEM300) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were collected by using TGA 4000-Frontier FTIR (Perkin Elmer) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha), respectively.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Duetta spectrophotometer (Horiba) at a steady state. An Edinburgh FLS-1000 photoluminescence spectrophotometer was used to measure the phosphorescence spectrum and afterglow decay curves of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite powders. Emission spectra of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution were also measured by a Dutta fluorescence and absorbance spectrometer with a slit width of 5 nm and integral time of 100 ms for both excitation and emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite-based WLEDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite powders (40 mg) were mixed with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) liquid (0.5 mL) and the obtained mixture was encapsulated on a commercial ultraviolet (UV) emitting light diode (LED) chip as a conversion layer [21]. Then the mixture on the UV-LED chip was cured in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h to produce WLEDs. The light-emitting spectra (340–800 nm) of the as-prepared WLEDs were collected by switching off the xenon lamp of the Duetta spectrophotometer.

Fig. 2 a displays that Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites are blocky-shaped particles. The EDS images (Fig. 2b–f) demonstrate that the Si, O, C, N and P elements are dispersed homogeneously in the composites (the corresponding contents are shown in Fig. S1), implying the Arg-CPDs are embedded in the SiO2 matrix. The morphology is further revealed by TEM images (Fig. 2g and Fig. S2), indicating the successful preparation of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 2h) shows the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite produces a wide diffraction peak at about 21.9° that is a typical amorphous carbon peak [22] and it suggests that their carbon cores consist of amorphous carbon.

The structures of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites are further explored by FTIR and XPS spectra in Fig. 3 a–e. A wide absorption peak between 2990–3670 cm−1 can be observed in Fig. 3a, which is ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds [23–25]. The absorption peaks at 799 cm−1 and 1065 cm−1 are respectively attributed to symmetric stretching vibration and asymmetric stretching vibration of the Si–O bonds, which are the results of the generation of the SiO2 network due to the hydrolysis of TEOS [26]. Furthermore, the absorption peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of CO and C–Si bonds, suggesting that Arg-CPDs have doped into the SiO2 network to generate the C–Si bond. This conclusion can be also supported by XPS spectra in Fig. 3b–e. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 3b) indicates that the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite is mainly composed of C, O and Si elements, from which the relative contents of C, O, and Si are around 3.72 %, 64.42 % and 30.74 %, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c) shows four Gaussian peaks at 284.3 eV, 285.3 eV, 286.3 eV and 287.7 eV, corresponding to C–Si, CC/C–C, C–N/C–O and CO, respectively [8,27]. For the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d), the three peaks at 531.7 eV, 533.1 eV and 534.0 eV are associated with CO, C–O and Si–O, respectively [28,29]. It illustrates that the Si–O groups are the primary oxygen-containing groups with a small amount of CO groups [29]. The Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) is deconvoluted into two different contributions at 103.0 eV, 103.7 eV and 104.4 eV, respectively referring to Si–C, Si–O–C and SiOx [26]. The XPS results confirm the formation of the C–Si bond in the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite, which is in agreement with the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3a). To facilitate the understanding of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite, the structure is schematically presented in Fig. 3f. Under the action of NH3·H2O, the C–Si bonds are created between the Arg-CPDs and the generated SiO2 network due to the TEOS hydrolysis, as confirmed by FTIR and XPS spectra. The SiO2 network can provide a rigid environment to restrict the movement of Arg-CPDs and protect them against oxygen and water, thus reducing the vibration of the surface functional groups and suppressing non-radiative transition. These characteristics facilitate the ISC for effectively populating triplet excitons [30] and eventually endow the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite with long-lived phosphorescence.

Fig. 4 a shows that the absorption spectrum has a major peak around 280 nm and a shoulder around 340 nm originating from π-π * and n-π * transitions of the CC and CO/CN groups in the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite that is analogous to the Arg-CPDs solution in previous work [19]. The Arg-CPDs@SiO2 aqueous solution exhibits strong blue fluorescence with maximal emission wavelength (Em) at 386 nm (Fig. 4a) under UV irradiation (excitation wavelength, Ex = 320 nm). Compared to the Arg-CPDs solution (Em = 445 nm, Ex = 365 nm), an apparent blue shift is revealed for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2, which is possibly owing to the rigid SiO2 network restricting the vibration of light-emitting groups. Fig. 4b shows an excitation-emission map of the aqueous solution exhibiting a narrow emission center, which is similar to that of the Arg-CPDs solution [19]. It suggests the photoluminescence properties of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution stem from Arg-CPDs in the solution.

Besides, an obvious afterglow can be observed in the aqueous Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution after switching off the UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 5 a, afterglow can last for 11 s by the naked eye. Fig. 5b shows the corresponding fluorescence (FL) and RTP spectra at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm at room temperature (298 K). The FL and RTP emission wavelengths are centered at 428 nm and 510 nm (the Stokes shift of 82 nm), demonstrating that afterglow is phosphorescence emission for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution. To further understand their RTP properties, Fig. 5c exhibits the time-resolved RTP decay spectrum at 365 nm excitation, fitting with the function
τ
a v
g
= Σ
α i
τ i 2
/
Σ
α i
τ i
. The fitting parameters are listed in Table S1 (supplementary material). It indicates the system possesses multiple decay channels, implying the existence of different electronic transition processes [8]. Besides, the average lifetime is calculated to be 881 ms. The long RTP lifetime in the aqueous solution can expand the application of RTP materials, especially in bioimaging and ion detection [1], since the presence of water and dissolved oxygen usually trigger phosphorescence quenching.

The anhydrous Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite powders can also exhibit remarkable afterglow after UV-light-excitation at 365 nm (Fig. S3). The intense blue fluorescent light is emitted when UV light is on. Subsequently, after the UV lamp is off, the RTP emission can be observed for about 10 s with the naked eye at room temperature. Fig. 6 a shows a UV–Vis absorption spectrum with a wide absorption between 250 nm and 500 nm for the composite, which is attributed to the π-π * and n-π * transitions of the CC and CO/CN groups. Fig. 6b displays that the FL and RTP emission wavelengths are 440 nm and 510 nm for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite under 365 nm excitation. Accordingly, the CIE coordinates of fluorescence and phosphorescence are calculated to be (0.20, 0.21) and (0.29, 0.39) in Fig. 6c and fall in the blue light and blue-green light regions, which is in accordance with the optical observation in Fig. S3. Fig. 6d shows the phosphorescence lifetime spectrum of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite and the corresponding fitting RTP decay curve of
τ
a v
g
= Σ
α i
τ i 2
/
Σ
α i
τ i
. The fitting parameters are listed in Table S2 and accordingly, the average lifetime is 543 ms for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite under 365 nm excitation. The cross-linked SiO2 network reduces the influence of external quencher on the Arg-CPDs in the composites. Furthermore, the formation of C–Si bonds between Arg-CPDs and SiO2 effectively inhibits the non-radiative transition process stemming from the molecular vibration of the light-emitting groups, and thus the radiative transition efficiency is improved, which is different from the quantum-size confinement of the nanoclusters in zeolite [31,32]. Therefore, the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite presents a long RTP lifetime (543 ms), which is more than 3 times higher than that of the Arg-CPDs materials (149 ms) [19].

Indeed, the luminescence mechanisms of CDs are very complicated and still not clear at present [33–35]. Three possible luminescence mechanisms of CDs are the quantum confinement effect (QCE), the surface state and the molecule state [35]. The QCE is mainly related to the conjugated π electrons and bandgap of conjugated π-domains; the surface state originates from the hybridization of the carbon backbone and the chemical groups or defects; and the molecule state is associated with the fluorescent molecules attached to the surface or interior of the carbon backbone [35–37]. The previous work [19] suggests the photoluminescence of Arg-CPDs stems from their surface sub-fluorophores (such as CO/CN groups), which is surface state emission. Therefore, the luminescence of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 is from the extrinsic surface states, since the SiO2 does not have luminescence properties in the present work. The SiO2 network can provide an important environment to prolong RTP lifetime of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites. That is to say, the RTP characteristics of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite originates from the cross-linked structure of the Arg-CPDs and their surface CO/CN groups of Arg-CPDs [19]. The SiO2 network and the formation of C–Si bonds restrict the vibration and rotation of these surface CO/CN groups and increase their rigidity, thus facilitating the ISC for effectively populating triplet excitons and suppressing non-radiative transition of triplet excitons [38]. Besides, the SiO2 network can provide a rigid environment to protect Arg-CPDs against the quenching agents (oxygen and water) and prevent the quenching of triplet excitons. Therefore, the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite in aqueous solution and solid state displays a longer RTP lifetime in than that of the Arg-CPDs.

Given that the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites possess the favorable luminescence properties, they are attempted to serve as a color conversion layer in LED prototypes. It emits white light when the Arg-CPDs@SiO2-based WLEDs work (inset of Fig. 7 a). Two emission peaks around 385 nm and 556 nm can be found for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2-based WLEDs in Fig. 7a. The emission peak at 385 nm originates from the intrinsic UV-LED chip, as confirmed by Fig. S4 exhibiting an identical peak. Therefore, the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite is responsible for the other peak at 556 nm. The CIE coordinates are calculated to be (0.38, 0.43) for the Arg-CPDs@SiO2-based WLEDs (Fig. 7b), which is close to an ideal pure white light with CIE coordinates (0.33, 0.33) [39,40]. Besides, the correlated color temperature (CCT) and color rendering index (CRI) are 4315 K and 74.6, respectively. The obtained light source is warm white light [41] that makes people feel warm, healthy and comfortable, and hence the Arg-CPDs@SiO2-based WLEDs may be favorable in families, restaurants, offices and so on [42].

The Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composite was prepared by embedding Arg-CPDs in the SiO2 matrix, where the C–Si bonds between the Arg-CPDs and SiO2 network were created. The composite exhibited an intense fluorescence emission after UV-light-excition at 365 nm and a long-lived phosphorescence emission of over 10 s can be observed by the naked eye. Besides, the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 solution also displayed a remarkable afterglow lifetime of over 10 s. The RTP lifetime was 543 ms for the anhydrous Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites, which was more than 3 times higher than that (149 ms) of the matrix-free Arg-CPDs materials. The rigid SiO2 network effectively inhibited the vibration of light-emitting groups of Arg-CPDs and isolated them for preventing the quenching of triplet excitons, which were responsible for the long-lived phosphorescence lifetime of Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites. Besides, a WLED was prepared by coating the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 powders on a UV-LED chip and it exhibited a favorable warm white light characteristic, demonstrating the promising potential of the Arg-CPDs@SiO2 composites in the field of illumination application. This work provided an effective method to improve the photoluminescence properties via introducing the rigid SiO2 network and therewith suggested the great application value in manufacturing the novel optical device based on the obtained composites.
Data will be made available on request.

Yichuan Zhang: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Qianhui Long: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Investigation. Yanjun Ding: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Chuan Huang: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation. Mingming Guo: Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. SWU-XDJH202314, SWU-KR22041 and SWU118113) and Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (No. CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0602). The authors would like to thank Tianyang Hu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and phosphorescence analysis.

Nat. Commun.
